rulings.Case Title: GILBERT PAPAZIAN II VS. KEVIN MICHAEL SULLIVAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Relieieving Pltf'S From Waiver Of Objections To Deft'S Req.'S For Written Discovery To Deft'S
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, JUNE 19, 2018, LINE 2 DEFENDANTS GRIFFIN & SULLIVAN and KEVIN SULLIVAN'S Motion For Order Relieieving Plaintiffs From Waiver Of Objections To Defendant's Request For Written Discovery Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. = SBS

rulings.Case Title: SEYEDEH KALANTARI VS. BLOOMINGDALE'S INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Bloomingdale'S Inc., Andre Lawrence Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2)
rulings.Rulings: Defendant Bloomingdale'S Inc., Andre Lawrence Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2) As to issue 6 regarding the after-acquired evidence doctrine/defense, there is a disputed issue about the consequences of Ms. Kalantari's removal of the detective reports. Her evidence shows that the reports were not confidential, they were routinely shared among detectives, and nobody had ever been fired for copying the reports. (Maykin Depo.189-190; Lawrence Depo., Lawrence Depo., 320-321). "Where an employer seeks to rely upon after-acquired evidence of wrongdoing, it must first establish that the wrongdoing was of such severity that the employee in fact would have been terminated on those grounds alone if the employer had known of it at the time of the discharge." (Thompson v. Tracor Flight Systems, Inc. (2001) 86 Cal.App.4th 1156, 1173.) Defendants' procedural objection to the manner in which Ms. Kalantari cited germane deposition testimony is overruled. The fact that Ms. Kalantari provided redacted pages does not render them inadmissible. Defendants fail to show how this mode of citation was materially misleading or compels a different result for the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Kalantari is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2)

rulings.Case Title: SEYEDEH KALANTARI VS. BLOOMINGDALE'S INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thuesday, June 19, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT BLOOMINGDALE'S INC., ANDRE LAWRENCE MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication. (Part 1 of 2). Defendant Bloomingdale's, Inc. and Andre Lawrence's motion for summary judgment or, in the alternative, motion for summary adjudication of issues is denied in its entirety. As to issues 1, 2, and 4, there are triable disputes whether the assertedly harassing conduct was sufficiently severe or pervasive to constitute sexual harassment and whether Bloomingdale's failed to prevent the harassment. The record is replete with offensive comments and Ms. Kalantari complained about them to Bloomingdale's. (Kalantari Depo. 105-129, 149, 151-153, 160-161, 167-168, 176-179; Jacobson Depo. 131-132; Rivera Depo. 53-55, 154-158).) The statements that Ms. Kalantari personally witnessed are sufficient to support a prima facie case of harassment. The statements that Mr. Lawrence made to co-workers can also contribute to the atmosphere of harassment. (Beyda v. City of Los Angeles (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 511, 521.) As to issues 3 and 5, there is a triable issue whether Ms. Kalantari was constructively terminated. There is evidence that after Ms. Kalantari engaged in her protected activity, Mr. Lawrence, Mr. Maykin, and Mr. Rogers began to treat her unfairly. On March 16, 2017, six days after her complaint, Bloomingdale's issued a formal disciplinary write-up for attendance issues. Bloomingdale's did so even though Ms. Kalantari was previously coached about the issue before her complaint (Kalantari depo. 190-191, 307) and co-workers were not similarly disciplined (Jacobson Depo. 176-177). Mr. Maykin also acknowledged that Ms. Kalantari's attendance had actually improved dramatically for the first quarter, which was immediately prior to the discipline. (Maykin Depo. 141.) Mr. Maykin unfairly criticized Ms. Kalantari's conduct related to the "nonproductive detainment" ("NPD"). (Kalantari Depo. 279-280). Mr. Maykin criticized Ms. Kalantari for hosting the post-NPD meeting although her supervisor, Mr. Lawrence, approved the meeting. (Kalantari's Depo. 286; Maykin Dec. Ex. B.) A reasonable person could find the situation intolerable and feel compelled to resign because of the nitpicking and/or trumped up charges. Ms. Kalantari reasonably feared for her safety given her managers' refusal to support her and the nature of her job, which sometimes involved altercations with members of the public. (Kalantari Depo. 330.) A trier of fact could also find a causal nexus between the protected activity and adverse employment action. There is a triable issue regarding Mr. Maykin's knowledge of Ms. Kalantari's protected conduct. Mr. Jacobson testified that Mr. Maykin admitted that he had knowledge of the HR investigation at the March 21, 2017 meeting. (Jacobson Depo. 163-164.) Mr. Jacobson also testified that Mr. Maykin told him that he wanted to get rid of Ms. Kalantari. (Jacobson Depo. 115-116 and 173.) End of Part 1 of 2 =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CHERYL LUNA VS. AVRAHAM SARANGA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thuesday, June 19, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT AVRAHAM SARANGA'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons. Specially appearing defendant Avraham Saranga's motion to quash service of summons and complaint is denied. Substituted service per CCP 415.20(b) was properly made on Mr. Saranga. Plaintiff Cheryl Luna acted with reasonable diligence in trying to personally serve Mr. Saranga by attempting service at his home address nine times, including three occasions when a guard confirmed that Mr. Saranga lived in the apartment where personal service was attempted. Although the process server did not subserve a competent household member, leaving the papers with the guard on duty constituted substantial compliance with the statutory requirement because 1) the server could not go upstairs to attempt service on a household member since the guard repeatedly refused the server access to the upstairs, 2) the guard works in the building where Mr. Saranga resides and is more likely than not to know Mr. Saranga, 3) the process server mailed a copy of the summons and complaint to the specified address after subserving the guard, and 4) Mr. Saranga has had actual notice about this case since February 2018, when he retained counsel. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Luna is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. CRITCHFIELD MECHANICAL, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thuesday, June 19, 2018, Line 8, PLAINTIFF CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT. Plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC's motion to strike the entirety of the cross-complaint filed by defendant Critchfield Mechanical, Inc., is granted in its entirety without prejudice to Critchfield seeking leave to file a cross-complaint that seeks relief against at least one non-fictiously named cross-defendant. No useful purpose is achieved by an indemnity cross-complaint against only fictiously named cross-defendants, Critchfield suffers no prejudice by the granting of this motion since the statute of limitations does not begin to run on any indemnity claim it may have until after liability has been found against it, and the pendency of the cross-complaint could delay this case to the prejudice of CDC San Francisco. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CDC San Francisco is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested =(302/HEK)
